claims.claim_text: In the recommendation war story, each recommendation call took roughly 500 ms.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Recommendation Catastrophe
claims.anchor_text: Each recommendation call took roughly 500 ms.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Each recommendation call added noticeable latency.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with precise latency detail, so the exact number should be removed rather than softened.

claims.claim_text: In the recommendation war story, the team precomputed recommendations hourly and stored them in Redis.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Recommendation Catastrophe
claims.anchor_text: The team precomputed recommendations hourly, stored them in Redis, and made the homepage perform a fast lookup.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The team moved recommendations out of the render path by precomputing them and serving a fast lookup.
claims.rationale: The hourly cadence and Redis implementation are fake-specific details in an unattributed story.

claims.claim_text: In the recommendation war story, latency dropped from user-visible seconds to a few milliseconds.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Recommendation Catastrophe
claims.anchor_text: Latency dropped from user-visible seconds to a few milliseconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Latency dropped enough that recommendation lookup was no longer the dominant page-render delay.
claims.rationale: The before-and-after latency range is precise operational detail from an unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: KServe is a Kubernetes-native model serving platform with multi-framework support, predictor/transformer/explainer routing, canary rollouts, autoscaling, scale-to-zero, explainability, and monitoring features.
claims.span_hint: sections: Framework Comparison; KServe: Kubernetes Serving
claims.rationale: The KServe README directly lists Kubernetes deployment, multi-framework support, predictor/transformer/explainer routing, canary rollouts, autoscaling, scale-to-zero, explainability, and monitoring.

claims.claim_text: KServe can split canary traffic using canaryTrafficPercent.
claims.rationale: The KServe canary rollout example documents canaryTrafficPercent and shows a 90/10 split between revisions.

claims.claim_text: KServe autoscaling fields such as minReplicas, maxReplicas, scaleTarget, and scaleMetric can be used for concurrency-based scaling.
claims.rationale: The KServe autoscaling sample documents minReplicas and concurrency behavior for InferenceService autoscaling.

claims.claim_text: Seldon Core supports Kubernetes model deployment, pipelines, autoscaling, multi-model serving, A/B tests, and shadow deployments.
claims.rationale: The Seldon Core README lists Kubernetes deployment, pipelines, autoscaling, multi-model serving, and experiments with A/B tests and shadow deployments.

claims.claim_text: BentoML builds model inference APIs, REST API servers, Docker images, dynamic batching, model parallelism, multi-stage pipelines, and multi-model inference-graph orchestration.
claims.span_hint: sections: Framework Comparison; Model Serving Frameworks
claims.rationale: The BentoML README directly describes REST APIs, Docker image generation, dynamic batching, model parallelism, pipelines, and multi-model orchestration.

claims.claim_text: TensorFlow Serving is a high-performance production serving system with TensorFlow integration, versioned model access, HTTP/gRPC endpoints, canarying, A/B testing, and batching.
claims.rationale: The TensorFlow Serving README directly lists these serving capabilities.

claims.claim_text: The framework comparison says TensorFlow Serving is TF only.
claims.anchor_text: | **TensorFlow Serving** | TensorFlow models | High performance | TF only |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **TensorFlow Serving** | TensorFlow-heavy serving | High performance, versioned serving | Most natural fit for TensorFlow/SavedModel workflows |
claims.rationale: The TensorFlow Serving README says it integrates with TensorFlow out of the box but can be extended to other model and data types, so 'TF only' is too absolute.

claims.claim_text: Triton supports multiple frameworks, concurrent model execution, dynamic batching, real-time/batched/streaming query types, and HTTP/REST and gRPC inference protocols.
claims.span_hint: sections: Framework Comparison; Optimization Techniques; Batching Example
claims.rationale: The Triton README directly lists multi-framework support, concurrent model execution, dynamic batching, query types, and HTTP/gRPC protocols.

claims.claim_text: The framework comparison says Triton has the best GPU utilization and is NVIDIA only.
claims.anchor_text: | **Triton** | Multi-framework GPU | Best GPU utilization | NVIDIA only |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Triton** | Multi-framework GPU and CPU inference | Dynamic batching, concurrent execution | NVIDIA-oriented operational stack |
claims.rationale: The superlative is uncited, and Triton documentation says it supports NVIDIA GPUs, x86 and ARM CPU, and AWS Inferentia rather than being strictly NVIDIA only.

claims.claim_text: ONNX provides a common/open model representation for interoperability across machine learning frameworks and tools.
claims.rationale: The ONNX README directly describes ONNX as an open format and open standard for machine learning interoperability.

claims.claim_text: TensorFlow Lite post-training float16 quantization can reduce model size and may reduce latency/power with little accuracy degradation.
claims.rationale: The claim is real and documented by TensorFlow, but tensorflow.org is not currently on the trusted-domain allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Netflix serves large-scale personalized recommendations using batch-computed embeddings used in offline and online applications.
claims.rationale: Netflix's own engineering blog supports the substance of the claim, but netflixtechblog.com is not on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The latency budget for real-time inference is often under 100 ms when embedded in a larger request path.
claims.anchor_text: - **The latency budget for real-time inference is often under 100 ms** when the model is embedded inside a larger user-facing request path that also includes network, feature lookup, and application logic.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **The latency budget for real-time inference can be tight** when the model is embedded inside a larger user-facing request path that also includes network, feature lookup, and application logic.
claims.rationale: The exact 'under 100 ms' threshold is a statistic without a primary source in the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Serving cost is often larger than training cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Optimization Is Production Work
claims.anchor_text: Serving cost is often larger than training cost.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Serving cost can become a major production expense.
claims.rationale: The training-versus-serving cost comparison is plausible but uncited and should not be stated as a general fact without a source.

claims.claim_text: Model serving costs can exceed training costs because training is periodic while inference services may run continuously.
claims.anchor_text: - **Model serving costs can exceed training costs** because training is periodic while successful inference services may run continuously at production traffic levels.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Model serving costs deserve ongoing attention** because training is periodic while successful inference services may run continuously at production traffic levels.
claims.rationale: This repeats the unsupported training-versus-serving cost comparison and should be rewritten unless a vetted source is added.

further_reading.title: KServe
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for Kubernetes-native model serving, InferenceService, autoscaling, canary rollouts, and multi-framework serving.

further_reading.title: Triton Inference Server
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for dynamic batching, concurrent model execution, multi-framework inference, and GPU-oriented serving operations.

further_reading.title: ONNX
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for ONNX as an open model format and interoperability standard.

further_reading.title: TensorFlow Serving
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for production TensorFlow model serving, versioned access, canarying, A/B testing, and batching.

notes: Most latency-budget tables and exercise scenarios are framed as hypothetical teaching examples and were not emitted as hard claims. Code blocks and Mermaid diagrams were skipped except where surrounding prose made explicit vendor capability claims.
